content.problemStatement:
Chef has a list of non-overlapping intervals intervalListintervalListintervalList sorted by their start times. Each interval is represented as a pair of integers indicating the start and end points. 

Now, Chef wants to insert a new interval into this list newIntervalValuenewIntervalValuenewIntervalValue. After inserting, Chef needs to ensure that the list remains sorted by start times and that no intervals overlap. 

If any intervals overlap due to the insertion, Chef wants to merge them into a single interval. Help Chef by writing a function that returns the updated list of merged intervals.

Function DeclarationFunction NameinsertIntervalinsertIntervalinsertInterval - This function inserts a new interval into a list of non-overlapping intervals and merges any overlapping intervals.

Parameters
intervalListintervalListintervalList: A array of intervals where each interval is represented as a array of two integers [start,end][start, end][start,end]. The intervals are initially non-overlapping and sorted by start time.
newIntervalValuenewIntervalValuenewIntervalValue: A array of two integers representing the new interval to insert into intervalListintervalListintervalList.
Return Value
Returns a 2D array of integers representing the updated list of merged intervals.
Each interval is represented as a array of two integers [start,end][start, end][start,end].
constraints: The intervals in intervalListintervalListintervalList are sorted by their start times in ascending order.
The first line contains an integer TTT — the number of test cases.

The first line contains array of integers representing Chef's intervals in the form: [[start1  end1],  [start2  end2],  …  [startN  endN]]start_1 \; end_1], \; [start_2 \; end_2], \; \ldots \; [start_N \; end_N]]start1​end1​],[start2​end2​],…[startN​endN​]].
The next line contains two integers representing Chef's new interval to insert.
For each test case, print the updated list of merged intervals after inserting the new interval.
The intervals should be sorted by their start times and must not overlap.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The new interval [5 10] does not overlap with [1 3] but overlaps with [6 9], so we merge [6 9] with [5 10].
Intervals [12 15] and [18 21] remain unchanged as they don't overlap with [5 10].

Explanation:
Insert [3 8] overlaps with [2 4] and [7 9], merging them into [2 9].
[12 14] remains separate as it does not overlap with the new interval.

Explanation:
The new interval [17 19] does not overlap with any existing intervals and comes after all of them, so it is simply appended at the end.

Explanation:
Insert [6 9] between [1 5] and [10 15] without overlap; intervals remain separate and sorted.

Explanation:
Insert [3 6] overlaps with [5 7], merge to [3 7].
[1 2] and [8 10] remain unchanged.
Final intervals: [1 2], [3 7], [8 10].

solution.timeComplexity: O(n), since we scan through the list once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n), due to the output list that may contain all intervals plus the new merged interval.

code.Python3:
class Solution:
    def insertInterval(self, intervalList, newIntervalValue):
        result = []
        i = 0
        n = len(intervalList)

        # 1. Add intervals ending before new interval starts
        while i < n and intervalList[i][1] < newIntervalValue[0]:
            result.append(intervalList[i])
            i += 1

        # 2. Merge overlapping intervals
        while i < n and intervalList[i][0] <= newIntervalValue[1]:
            newIntervalValue[0] = min(newIntervalValue[0], intervalList[i][0])
            newIntervalValue[1] = max(newIntervalValue[1], intervalList[i][1])
            i += 1

        # Add merged interval
        result.append(newIntervalValue)

        # 3. Add the rest
        while i < n:
            result.append(intervalList[i])
            i += 1

        return result
